title: Tapping for Spiritual but Not Religious
subtitle: An EFT sequence for the discomfort of not having a named framework for what you believe
excerpt: An EFT tapping practice for the uncomfortable middle ground of believing in something but not fitting any organised tradition. Works through the lack of a named framework, the sense of being untethered from community, and toward a settled acceptance of a personal spiritual position.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. EFT framework developed by Gary Craig (mid-1990s).

practice.whenToUse: Use when you feel caught between a belief in something beyond the material and the absence of a tradition or community that holds that belief alongside you.

content.text: A five-minute EFT tapping practice for the uncomfortable position of having a spiritual life without a named tradition. The script works through the lack of a container and the sense of not fully belonging anywhere, toward a place where a personal, unnamed spiritual position is sufficient.
New to tapping? Read
how tapping works
first.
The practice
Karate chop
Tap the karate chop point and say each statement once:
Even though I believe in something but don't have a name for it, I deeply and completely accept myself.
Even though I don't fit neatly into any tradition and that sometimes feels like a gap, I am open to my own path being enough.
Even though I sometimes feel spiritually homeless, I accept that my experience of what I believe is real.
Tapping round
Tap each point seven times while saying the phrase:
Eyebrow: I believe in something. I just can't name it neatly.
Side of Eye: I don't fit the religious category or the atheist one.
Under Eye: The loneliness of a spiritual life without a community for it.
Under Nose: The feeling that a real belief would have a proper framework.
Chin: What I believe is real but I can't point at it on a shelf.
Collarbone: The discomfort of not quite belonging anywhere spiritually.
Under Arm: Wishing someone had built a tradition that fit what I already feel.
Top of Head: I am making this up as I go. That feels unstable.
Reframe to positive
Eyebrow: My belief doesn't need a name to be real.
Side of Eye: Many people hold what they hold without a tradition.
Under Eye: My experience of something larger is its own kind of data.
Under Nose: I can have a practice without having a doctrine.
Chin: The framework is mine to build. That is not a flaw.
Collarbone: I belong to my own spiritual life. That is enough.
Under Arm: I am allowed to be certain about what I feel and uncertain about the framework.
Top of Head: My path is mine. I follow it.
Tap each round three times, then move to the reframe and tap that three times.
Where this practice comes from
Written for homemade.education. The EFT framework is Gary Craig's Emotional Freedom Technique, in use since the mid-1990s.
